turns.content: I keep thinking of this like a stack where you can look at the top element without popping it, but I've never seen that pattern in Rust before. Is there a trait or type that lets me peek ahead in an iterator without consuming tokens?

Yes, you are looking for the Peekable trait from the itertools crate which wraps any iterator and provides methods like next() to consume elements and peek() to inspect them temporarily. For example, if you have an iterator over strings you can wrap it in Peekable<Strings> so that peek() returns the current token without removing it from the stream.
Wait, I thought iterators just moved forward automatically? How does wrapping them help if they still advance when you call next()? Isn't this redundant?

That is a common misunderstanding because the underlying iterator still advances after next() but peek() specifically checks if the current element matches what was expected before calling next(). This allows you to implement one-token-lookahead parsers where you can reject malformed input by checking the lookahead token without actually consuming it from your lexing buffer.
So if I use Peekable in a hand-written lexer, I can skip tokens that don't match my grammar rules? Like if I see a number but expect an identifier?

Exactly, you can peek at the next token using peek() to validate context before committing to consuming it with next(). For instance in a Rust lexer for expressions you might check if the current token is a digit or operator by peeking ahead and only calling next() once the lookahead confirms the token belongs in your current parse state.
I'm confused about how this differs from just reading tokens sequentially. Why do I need to manually implement this logic instead of relying on Rust's standard iterator behavior?

Standard iterators only expose next() which forces consumption, but Peekable adds the extra peek() method that lets you inspect without advancing. This is essential for parsers because you often need to look ahead multiple positions in a token stream to determine grammar rules before deciding whether to consume tokens or backtrack.
Can I chain multiple peek() calls together? Like looking at two tokens ahead to check for operator precedence without consuming either of them?

You can call peek() repeatedly on the same Peekable instance as long as you don't consume anything in between, but if you accidentally call next() once it invalidates all future peeks. For complex lookahead you might need to implement a custom parser combinator that wraps Peekable and exposes multiple lookahead methods.

I'm trying to build a simple lexer for my first project using the itertools crate, but I keep thinking I can just use a standard iterator and check the next token before moving forward. My assumption is that if I call next() twice in a row it will give me two tokens without consuming either one.

That won't work because iterators are designed to consume elements as they are yielded; calling next() advances the state so you cannot retrieve the same element again without advancing. To achieve lookahead behavior, you need to use the Peekable trait which wraps your iterator and allows you to inspect upcoming tokens without consuming them.
I see, so I should wrap my token stream with a Peekable instance instead of just using the raw iterator. But how do I actually call the peek method since it seems like it returns an Option?

The peek method on a Peekable wrapper returns an Option containing the next element if available, or None if there are no more tokens to read. You check this Option and only consume via next() when you're sure about what token is coming.

Okay, I'm implementing a basic identifier parser now with the Peekable wrapper around my token iterator. My current logic checks if the peek returns Some(token) and then consumes it to build an identifier string.

That's correct for building identifiers, but be careful about operator precedence in your parsing logic; sometimes you need to check multiple tokens before deciding whether to consume them all. For example, when detecting if a sequence of characters is actually a number or a variable name depending on the following token.
Ah, I was ignoring that edge case! So my lexer needs to peek at the token after an operator to decide if it's part of a larger construct like a function call. That makes more sense now.

Exactly, you should implement a helper method in your parser that uses the Peekable wrapper to look ahead two or three tokens before committing to consume them, which prevents premature consumption errors.

Imagine I'm building a custom lexer for a tiny DSL and my current approach to parsing fails because I consume tokens too eagerly. Can you help me understand how to add one-token lookahead without modifying the original iterator?

That's exactly the problem with standard iterators; they are stateless and once an item is yielded, it is gone forever unless you collect them into a Vec first. To solve this without mutating your input stream, you need to wrap the iterator in a Peekable structure. The solution is to use the Peekable trait from std::iter which provides methods like peek() that let you inspect the next element without taking it.
So I just call .peekable() on my iterator? Does that create a new collection or does it wrap the existing one?

It wraps the existing one without creating a separate collection. You take your source iterator and call .peekable() to get an impl Iterator that implements Peekable, then you can use methods like peek(), next(), and consume(). This lets you look ahead by calling peek() once before consuming with next().
I think I understand the basic flow now. But wait, does peek() actually change the iterator state? Or is it purely read-only?

That is a great question and you are right to double-check because many new developers assume peek() might consume or buffer data internally. The key insight is that peek() never advances the iterator; it only checks what would be returned by next(). However, if you call consume(n), that does advance the iterator by n items so your logic must handle both read-only inspection and stateful consumption carefully.

Okay, so I can peek multiple times in a row as long as I don't call next() or consume() until I'm ready to commit. That makes sense for my lookahead logic. One last thing though: what happens if the iterator is empty and I call peek()? Is that safe?

Calling peek() on an exhausted iterator returns None, so it is not unsafe but you must handle the Optional result in your code. If you are parsing tokens and get a None from peek(), you should probably return early or raise a specific error instead of trying to consume more items.

I'm working on a lexer for our project and I have my own Iterator implementation that yields tokens. It works fine but I was wondering if there's a better way to handle lookahead without consuming the next token. My current approach just calls next() twice which feels inefficient.

You are likely dealing with iterator consumption, where calling next() advances the underlying stream permanently. A better pattern is to use Peekable from std::io or a custom wrapper that implements PeekIterator so you can peek at tokens without moving them forward in your lexer.
That sounds promising but I'm not sure how to apply it. Can you walk me through the exact steps of implementing a Peekable iterator for my custom token stream?

You should create a struct that wraps your iterator and implements Iterator, then use PeekIterator from std::io or implement your own Peek trait. This allows you to peek at the next token without consuming it in your hand-written parser logic.
I think I understand now but I want to make sure this is efficient for large files. Is there any overhead with using Peekable compared to just calling next() twice?"

Using a Peekable implementation avoids redundant buffer reads, so it's more efficient than calling next() twice because you don't re-parse or re-read the same token. This is especially important for large files where performance matters.
